FT_IC1.evidence: Cross-sectional baseline (2016-2018) data from Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study; n=10,755; U.S. ABCD population confirmed.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: "Multiple linear regression analyses were conducted to estimate associations between sociodemographic factors and 6 contemporary forms of screen time."

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Pediatrics (2022; 240:213-20).

FT_IC2.evidence: All analyses pair screen time modalities (TV, videos, video games, social networking, texting, video chat) exclusively with demographic-only predictors (sex, race/ethnicity, country of birth, household income, parental education). No individual-trait-level variables (behavioral, health, cognitive, well-being, personality, substance use, sleep, academic, psychiatric, ACEs, parenting, family functioning) on either side. Criteria explicitly require other-side to be individual-trait-level; demographic-only fails FT-IC2.

notes: Paper is a sociodemographic correlates study of screen time prevalence. No connection to individual-trait outcomes.

FT_IC1.evidence: December 2020 COVID-19 survey from Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study; n=4,106 U.S. adolescents ages 12-14.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: "Modified Poisson regression analyses with coefficients transformed to risk ratios were used to estimate the associations between COVID-19 information and masking behaviors."

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Preventive Medicine Reports (2022; 28:101900).

FT_IC2.evidence: Independent variables are contextual-only information sources (frequency of checking COVID-19 information on social media, internet, television; parental counseling). Dependent variable is masking behavior (health behavior compliance). Criteria state "Contextual-only...on the other side → NOT_MET." Information/media exposure and parental counseling are contextual sources, not individual-trait characteristics. Paper is about media exposure → behavioral compliance, not SM/phone use paired with individual-trait outcomes.

notes: Study examines impact of COVID information sources on masking behavior; information exposure is contextual, not individual-trait-level outcome.

FT_IC1.evidence: Cross-sectional data from Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study Year 2 (2018-2020); n=8,753 children ages 10-14; U.S. ABCD confirmed.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: "Multiple linear regression analyses were used to estimate associations between sociodemographic factors and adolescent-reported problematic video game (VGAQ), social media (SMAQ), and mobile phone use (MPIQ)."

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Pediatric Research (2022; 92:1443-1449).

FT_IC2.evidence: BINDING clarification §2.4: "Problematic-use scales are SM/phone variables, not 'individual-trait' outcomes. SMAQ, MPIQ, VGAQ...are SM/phone variables. When the only analysis is sociodemographic predictor → SMAQ/MPIQ as DV, the other side is still demographic — FT-IC2 = NOT_MET." Paper analyzes sociodemographic predictors (age, sex, race/ethnicity, primary language, household income, parental education, parent marital status) paired with VGAQ, SMAQ, and MPIQ scores as dependent variables. These scales are classified as SM/phone variables, not individual-trait outcomes. The other side is demographic-only.

notes: Paper examines sociodemographic correlates of problematic screen use scales (VGAQ, SMAQ, MPIQ). Problematic-use scales are classified as SM/phone variables, not individual-trait outcomes; demographic predictors fail FT-IC2.
